card.hook: An MV3 security extension can still observe wallet signing requests, but for most consumer Chrome extensions it cannot rely on arbitrary in-flight blocking, while the approval surface now spans EIP-712 signatures, EIP-2718/EIP-1559 transactions, and ERC-4337 UserOperations. That makes a single approval-time verdict a product and architecture problem, not just a decoding problem.
card.why_it_matters: If you're learning to build browser-extension security tooling for smart-contract workflows, this defines the minimum architecture you need to inspect modern Ethereum approvals under real MV3 and store-policy constraints.
card.action: Write a one-page decision note that lists the exact signing surfaces your extension will support first—eth_sendTransaction, personal_sign, eth_signTypedData, and ERC-4337/ERC-7769 UserOperation-related flows—and for each one record whether your MV3 design will observe, decode, or block it.
